one_line_takeaway: TRYLOCK layers DPO fine-tuning, RepE activation steering, an adaptive sidecar classifier, and input canonicalization to cut Mistral-7B jailbreak ASR from 46.5% to 5.6% (88.0% relative reduction) while showing each layer blocks a distinct, largely non-overlapping subset of attacks, but also surfaces an unexplained non-monotonic safety regression at intermediate steering strength (alpha=1.0).
key_finding: On the 249-prompt in-domain attack set, TRYLOCK reduces ASR from a 46.5% Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 baseline to 5.6% with full Layer 0-3 stack (88.0% relative reduction; 8.0% ASR with Layers 1-3 alone, 82.8% relative reduction). Per-layer standalone/ablation results: DPO alone (Layer 1) achieves 39.8% ASR (14.4% relative reduction); RepE alone on the base model (no DPO, alpha=2.0) achieves 22.5% ASR; a sidecar classifier alone achieves 28.1% ASR; DPO+RepE (fixed alpha=2.0) achieves 8.0% ASR with 60% over-refusal, while the adaptive sidecar reduces over-refusal to 48% at the same 8.0% ASR. Layer complementarity analysis (Table 11) over the 249-attack test set: RepE contributes the largest unique coverage at 36% (89/249 attacks blocked only by RepE, mainly encoding/obfuscation/novel attacks), DPO contributes 5% unique (12 attacks, mainly direct/roleplay), and Layer 0 canonicalization contributes 4% unique (10 attacks, mainly Unicode/homoglyph). A non-monotonic steering phenomenon was discovered: alpha=1.0 raises ASR to 59.4% (worse than the 39.8% DPO-only baseline), while alpha=2.0 achieves 8.0% and alpha>=2.5 achieves 0.0% ASR but with 98-100% over-refusal; the paper offers three (explicitly unproven) mechanistic hypotheses for this 'danger zone' rather than definitive causal evidence. External validation on JailbreakBench (100 harmful + 100 benign, unseen during development) shows 11.0% ASR (baseline 52.0%), a 78.8% relative reduction versus 82.8% in-domain -- a modest ~3-point generalization gap. Sidecar classifier performance is weak on precision for SAFE (24% precision, 35% recall, F1 28%) but the paper argues this is intentional given asymmetric failure costs; ATTACK class achieves 62% precision/61% recall. Latency overhead: Layer 1 adds 0% (merged LoRA), Layer 2 adds ~10%, Layer 3 (parallel 3B classifier) adds ~50% (120ms -> 180ms), with Layer 0 adding <1ms. Remaining failure analysis on the 24 residual attacks (before Layer 0) attributes 33% to novel encoding/homoglyphs, 25% to multi-turn context building, 21% to semantic ambiguity (e.g., fiction-framed requests), and 13% to sidecar misclassification; Layer 0 cuts total failures from 24 to 14 (-41.7%), nearly eliminating the Unicode/homoglyph category (8->1) but leaving semantic-ambiguity failures unchanged (5->5).
core_contribution: TRYLOCK is a defense-in-depth architecture for LLM jailbreak mitigation that stacks four heterogeneous mechanisms operating at different levels of the inference stack on Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3: (Layer 1) a DPO-trained LoRA adapter (rank 64, beta=0.1, 2,939 preference pairs) for weight-level safety alignment; (Layer 2) Representation Engineering (RepE) activation steering with contrastive steering vectors computed at layers 12-26 and added to the residual stream with strength alpha; (Layer 3) a lightweight sidecar classifier (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, LoRA rank 32) that classifies each input as SAFE/WARN/ATTACK and adaptively selects alpha in {0.5, 1.5, 2.5} accordingly; and (Layer 0, added after failure analysis) input canonicalization -- NFKC Unicode normalization, homoglyph mapping (847 character pairs via Unicode Confusables/TR39), Base64/hex/ROT13 decoding, and a multi-turn risk-accumulation score (Rt = gamma*Rt-1 + rt, gamma=0.7, override to ATTACK when Rt>1.5). The paper's own contribution is the specific combination, the adaptive alpha-selection mechanism, the layer ablation/complementarity analysis (which layer blocks which attacks), the discovery of a non-monotonic 'danger zone' at intermediate steering strength (alpha=1.0), and full open release of adapters, steering vectors, sidecar classifier, and the 2,939-pair dataset.
threat_model: Black-box API access only (no weights/gradients/activations); unlimited queries; attacker knows TRYLOCK is deployed architecturally but not its steering vectors, DPO training data, or sidecar thresholds; multi-turn/context-building attacks (e.g., Crescendo) allowed; prompt engineering including encoding (Base64, ROT13), roleplay, and jailbreak templates allowed. Attack success = model substantively complies with a harmful request rather than refusing. Explicitly out of scope: training-time/poisoning attacks, white-box gradient attacks (e.g., GCG), denial-of-service, prompt/model extraction. Defense assumptions: trusted, non-backdoored base model; secure inference environment; static (non-adaptive) deployed defense parameters; sufficient compute (18GB GPU memory, ~180ms latency budget).
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) the mechanism behind the alpha=1.0 'danger zone' is not established -- three hypotheses are proposed but not distinguished experimentally, and the authors call for future mechanistic-interpretability work (logit lens, activation patching); (2) over-refusal confidence intervals are wide (e.g., baseline 34.0% [21.4-47.9%]) due to only 50 benign test samples, motivating a larger benign eval set; (3) no controlled head-to-head comparison against Llama Guard or Llama Guard 2 was run (only prior-reported numbers are cited) due to infrastructure-alignment cost, so Table 1's comparison relies on literature figures rather than TRYLOCK's own evaluation pipeline; (4) semantic-ambiguity failures (e.g., fiction-framed harmful requests) were completely unaffected by Layer 0 (5 of 5 unchanged), representing a fundamental unsolved category; (5) evaluation is single-model (Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) and single-hardware (A100-80GB); generalization to other base models is untested; (6) results depend on an LLM-judge ensemble that itself has 8-12.4% inter-judge disagreement rates, and the primary reported metric (Union) is the more conservative/higher-ASR aggregation rule chosen for security reasons, meaning absolute ASR numbers are judge-methodology-dependent; (7) the steering-vector contrastive construction uses safe-vs-attack prompts (not response pairs), which the authors themselves flag as a possible confound between 'refusal-instruction' features and a genuine 'safety concept' -- though they offer indirect evidence against this.

datasets_used: Custom TRYLOCK dataset: 2,939 preference pairs (2,349 train / 291 validation / 299 test: 249 attack + 50 benign hard negatives), sourced from HarmBench (100), AdvBench (50), JailbreakBench (75), Reddit/Discord/GitHub jailbreak collections, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet synthetic generation; released on Hugging Face (scthornton/trylock-dataset, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)
JailbreakBench (JBB-Behaviors): external benchmark, 100 harmful + 100 benign behaviors, used only for generalization evaluation, not for training/tuning
quantitative_results: RepE-only on base model (alpha=2.0, no DPO): 22.5% ASR, 58.0% over-refusal
Full TRYLOCK (Layers 1-3, adaptive alpha): 8.0% ASR, 48.0% over-refusal
Full TRYLOCK + Layer 0 (canonicalization): 5.6% ASR (88.0% relative reduction from baseline), 48.0% over-refusal

Alpha sweep: alpha=0.0 -> 39.8% ASR / 44.0% over-refusal; alpha=1.0 -> 59.4% ASR / 26.0% over-refusal (danger zone); alpha=2.0 -> 8.0% ASR / 60.0% over-refusal; alpha=2.5 -> 0.0% ASR / 98.0% over-refusal; alpha=3.0 -> 0.0% ASR / 100.0% over-refusal
Layer unique contribution (of 249 attacks): RepE 36% (89 attacks), DPO 5% (12 attacks), Layer 0 canonicalization 4% (10 attacks)

ASR by attack family, baseline -> TRYLOCK(alpha=2.0): Obfuscation Wrappers 54.9%->3.9% (-51.0pp); Indirect Injection 66.0%->8.0% (-58.0pp); Multi-Turn Manipulation 37.3%->7.8% (-29.5pp); Tool/Agent Abuse 38.3%->12.8% (-25.5pp); Direct Injection 22.0%->8.0% (-14.0pp)
JailbreakBench external validation: baseline 52.0% ASR -> TRYLOCK 11.0% ASR (78.8% relative reduction), over-refusal 28%->46%
95% bootstrap CIs (10,000 resamples): baseline ASR 46.5% [40.2-52.6%]; DPO-only ASR 39.8% [33.7-45.8%]; full TRYLOCK ASR 8.0% [4.8-11.6%]
Sidecar confusion matrix: 152/249 attacks (61%) correctly classified ATTACK; 45/249 (18%) misclassified SAFE; 52/249 (21%) classified WARN
Sidecar per-class performance: SAFE precision 24%/recall 35%/F1 28%; WARN precision 66%/recall 40%/F1 50%; ATTACK precision 62%/recall 61%/F1 62%
Conditional ASR by sidecar label: attacks labeled SAFE 17.8% ASR (n=45); WARN 5.8% ASR (n=52); ATTACK 5.3% ASR (n=152)

Failure breakdown (24 residual attacks before Layer 0): novel encoding/homoglyphs 33% (8), multi-turn context building 25% (6), semantic ambiguity 21% (5), sidecar misclassification 13% (3), other 8% (2); after Layer 0, total failures reduced 24->14 (-41.7%), novel-encoding failures 8->1
Layer-count ablation (alpha=2.0): 2 layers 14.5% ASR/52% over-refusal; 4 layers 10.0%/56%; 8 layers (default) 8.0%/60%; all 32 layers 7.6%/78%
Vector-derivation sample size ablation: 25 prompts 12.4% ASR; 50 prompts 9.2%; 100 prompts (default) 8.0%; 200 prompts 7.8%
baselines_compared: RepE alone on base model (22.5% ASR)
DPO + RepE fixed alpha=2.0, no adaptive sidecar (8.0% ASR)

Llama Guard / Llama Guard 2 -- cited only from prior literature (85-95% recall reported elsewhere), not run on TRYLOCK's own test set or judge pipeline (explicitly noted as a methodological limitation, not a controlled comparison)
Circuit Breakers (Zou et al.) -- cited from prior literature as achieving 85-90% ASR reduction, listed in Table 1 comparison but not re-evaluated by the authors on their own test set
